prompt: Which algorithm is the appropriate choice for storing user passwords?

options.text: Argon2 (or bcrypt / scrypt) — slow and salted by design

explanation: Password storage needs a deliberately slow, salted KDF — Argon2, bcrypt, or scrypt — to resist GPU brute force (CWE-916). SHA-256/MD5 are far too fast; AES is reversible encryption, the wrong tool for one-way password storage.
source_reference: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet; CWE-916

prompt: Why does a password hash need a unique per-user salt?

options.text: So identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow tables
To make the hash reversible for password recovery
To encrypt the password in transit

explanation: A unique salt means two users with the same password get different stored hashes, which defeats rainbow tables and forces per-account cracking. Salts do not make hashes reversible, compress them, or handle transport security (that is TLS).
source_reference: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet; CWE-916

prompt: What is the purpose of the 'work factor' / cost parameter in bcrypt or Argon2?

options.text: To make each hash deliberately expensive, slowing brute-force attacks (and raise it as hardware improves)
To remove the need for a salt

explanation: The cost/work factor sets how much CPU/memory each hash takes, so attackers can only try few guesses per second; you increase it over time as hardware gets faster. It does not shorten the hash, reverse it, or replace the salt.
source_reference: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet; CWE-916
